request.question: Emma Hamilton is an Austarlian-born actress and graduate of the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art which is based in what city?

title: Eva Simatou
Antonia Kinlay
Estelle Kohler
Royal Academy of Dramatic Art
Katherine Kelly (actress)
Hugh Sinclair (actor)
Emma Hamilton (film)
Emma Hamilton (actress)
Tony Beckley
Tomahawk Theatre Company
sentences: Eva Simatou is a Greek actress.
She is a first class graduate of the Advanced Drama School Theatro Technis Karolos Koun (Athens Art Theatre).
She has also studied in London at City University and holds a BSc in Sociology and Media Studies and an MA in Arts Management & Criticism.
She was accepted by the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art (RADA) and has successfully completed the Contemporary Drama programme.

She is a graduate of the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art In the West End, Kinlay has played Melody in Michael Longhurst's production of "Bad Jews" at the Theatre Royal Haymarket and Penny in "Three Lions" at the St James Theatre.
In 2016 she appeared in "The Suicide" at the National Theatre and played the title role in "Lady Anna: All at Sea" at the Park Theatre
Estelle Kohler (born 28 March 1940) is a British theatre and television actress.
Born in South Africa, Kohler made a name for herself as a Shakespearean actor in England.

She is a graduate of Royal Academy of Dramatic Art, of which she is an Associate Member, and was nominated a Laurence Olivier Award in 2000 for her performance as Paulina in the Barbican Centre's production of "The Winter's Tale" (1999 season).
She is also remembered for her performance as Hilary Nash (later Main) in the TV series "The Main Chance", opposite John Stride.
Her voice is featured in several audio books by Naxos Audiobooks.
The Royal Academy of Dramatic Art (RADA) is a drama school in London, England that provides training for film, television and theatre.
It is one of the oldest and most prestigious drama schools in the United Kingdom, founded in 1904 by Herbert Beerbohm Tree.
Katherine Kelly (born 19 November 1979) is an English actress.

A 2001 graduate of the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art, she played Becky McDonald in the ITV soap opera "Coronation Street" from 2006 to 2012, for which she won Best Actress at the 2009 British Soap Awards and Best Serial Drama Performance at the 2012 National Television Awards.
Since 2013, she has starred as Mae, Lady Loxley in the ITV drama series "Mr Selfridge".
Hugh Sinclair (19 May 1903 – 29 December 1962) was a British actor born in London, England, the son of a clergyman.
He was educated at Charterhouse School and a graduate of the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art.
His first marriage was to the actress Valerie Taylor.
In his book "The Stage Struck Me!"
fellow actor Neville Phillips felt Sinclair always played variations of himself, handsome, debonair, suave and witty and excelled in light comedy.
By contrast Phillips felt his wife, who Sinclair often appeared opposite, was a dramatic actress of tremendous power with a magnificent voice.
Emma Hamilton is a 1968 historical drama film directed by Christian-Jaque and starring Michèle Mercier, Richard Johnson and John Mills.
It was partly based on the 1864 novel "La Sanfelice" by Alexandre Dumas and depicts the love affair between Emma Hamilton and Horatio Nelson.
It was a co-production between Italy, West Germany, France and the United States.
Emma Hamilton (born 1984 in Melbourne) is an Australian-born British film, television and theatre actress and graduate of the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art.
Derek Anthony "Tony" Beckley (7 October 1929 – 19 April 1980) was an English character actor.

A graduate of the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art, Beckley went on to carve out a career on film and television throughout the 1960s and 1970s often playing villainous roles, as well as being a veteran of numerous stage productions.
Tomahawk is a theatre company based in Oxford, South East England.

Founded in 2005 by Alex Nicholls and Oliver Baird among others, experienced personnel work alongside young actors, directors and technicians, many of whom have gone on to train in London at the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art (RADA), London Academy of Music and Dramatic Art (LAMDA) and Mountview Academy of Theatre Arts.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Emma Hamilton is an Austarlian-born actress and graduate of the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art which is based in what city?
hop1_docs: Emma Hamilton (actress) | Emma Hamilton (born 1984 in Melbourne) is an Australian-born British film, television and theatre actress and graduate of the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art.

Barbara Ewing | Barbara Ewing (born 1944) is a UK-based actress, playwright and novelist.  Born in New Zealand, she graduated from Victoria University of Wellington with a BA in English and Maori before moving to Britain in 1965 to train as an actress at RADA (the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art) in London.

Barbara Bryne | Barbara Bryne (born 1 April 1929) is a British-born U.S.-based stage, film and television actress.  Onstage she has appeared in comedy, dramatic and musical productions.  The London-born actress studied at the Royal Academy of Dramatic Arts (RADA).

Victoria Hamilton | Victoria Hamilton (born 1971) is an English actress.  After training at the London Academy of Music and Dramatic Art, Hamilton began her career in classical theatre, appearing in productions with the Royal Shakespeare Company and the National Theatre.  In 2002, she appeared in the London stage play "A Day in the Death of Joe Egg" alongside Clive Owen and later Eddie Izzard.
Lucy Davenport | Lucy Davenport is a British actress who graduated at the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art in 1999.
Kirsty Besterman | Kirsty Besterman is a British actress of the stage and screen.  She trained at the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art graduating in 2002.

Hannah Arterton | Hannah Jane Arterton (born 26 January 1989) is an English actress.  She was born in Gravesend, Kent.  Arterton attended Gravesend Grammar School for Girls and graduated from the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art in 2011.  Her feature film debut was the romantic musical "Walking on Sunshine" (2014).  She is the younger sister of actress Gemma Arterton.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
1) Evidence found:
- Emma Hamilton: passage [1] lists "Emma Hamilton (born 1984 in Melbourne)" and notes she is a "graduate of the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art." (passage [1])
- Royal Academy of Dramatic Art (RADA): passage [2] states "RADA (the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art) in London." (passage [2]) Passage [3] also refers to "Royal Academy of Dramatic Arts (RADA)" (passage [3]).

2) Ambiguities:
- RADA name variants appear: "Royal Academy of Dramatic Art" (passage [1],[2]) vs "Royal Academy of Dramatic Arts" (passage [3]) and the abbreviation "RADA". 
- Only one Emma Hamilton page appears in the passages ("Emma Hamilton (actress)" in passage [1]); no other Emma Hamilton variants are present in these passages.

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entity: "Royal Academy of Dramatic Art" (as named in the passages).
- Precise missing fact needed: authoritative confirmation of the city in which RADA is based (the item's canonical location as shown in RADA's lede/infobox).

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized next hops):
- Open "Royal Academy of Dramatic Art" (RADA) page — check the lede and infobox for "Location/City" and the History/Overview section to confirm the city. (Search: "Royal Academy of Dramatic Art lede infobox location")
- Open "Emma Hamilton (actress)" page — check the lede/infobox or Education/Career section to confirm she graduated from RADA. (Search: "Emma Hamilton (actress) RADA graduate lede")
- If RADA page shows variant naming or a disambiguation, fetch "RADA (disambiguation)" or the specific institutional page titled with the city's name and check the same sections.
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved and verified.
response.hop2_query: Royal Academy of Dramatic Art location city
hop2_docs: Maastricht Academy of Dramatic Arts | The Maastricht Academy of Dramatic Arts (Dutch: "Toneelacademie Maastricht") is a college of dramatic arts located in the city of Maastricht in the Netherlands.  It is part of the Zuyd University (Hogeschool Zuyd).  The school is housed in a former Protestant orphanage in the historic Jekerkwartier in Maastricht.

Royal Academy of Dramatic Art | The Royal Academy of Dramatic Art (RADA) is a drama school in London, England that provides training for film, television and theatre.  It is one of the oldest and most prestigious drama schools in the United Kingdom, founded in 1904 by Herbert Beerbohm Tree.
American Musical and Dramatic Academy | The American Musical and Dramatic Academy (AMDA) is a college conservatory for the performing arts located in New York City and Los Angeles, California.

Tomahawk Theatre Company | Tomahawk is a theatre company based in Oxford, South East England.  Founded in 2005 by Alex Nicholls and Oliver Baird among others, experienced personnel work alongside young actors, directors and technicians, many of whom have gone on to train in London at the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art (RADA), London Academy of Music and Dramatic Art (LAMDA) and Mountview Academy of Theatre Arts.

Stockholm Academy of Dramatic Arts | On January 1, 2011 the two academies Dramatiska institutet (the Dramatic Institute/University College of Film, Radio, Television and Theatre) and Teaterhögskolan i Stockholm (Swedish National Academy of Dramatic Arts/Stockholm Academy of Dramatic Arts) founded a new academy: Stockholms dramatiska högskola, Stockholm Academy of Dramatic Arts (SADA).

Mehdi Forough | Mehdi Forough (1911–2008) was an Iranian scholar, author, dramatist, writer on dramatic arts and culture, translator, and founder of the Academy of Dramatic Arts in Tehran.  A native of Esfahan, Mehdi Forough attended Danesh Sara-ye Aali in Tehran and went on to study at the Royal Academy of Dramatic Arts, London.  He did graduate studies at Columbia University, NYC, where he completed his thesis, "Comparative study of Abraham's sacrifice in Persian passion plays and Western mystery plays", 1954.  This work was later published by the Ministry of Culture, in Tehran.
Shereen Martineau | Martineau trained at the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art (RADA).
response.summary_2: RADA (the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art) in London. Emma Hamilton (born 1984 in Melbourne) is listed as a graduate of the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art.
